title: Fighting against triple threat
full_text:
The impacts of climate change are increasing infrastructure and service costs for councils, heightening calls for a dedicated climate compensation fund to protect ratepayers.
Hobart City Council has become the seventh in the country to back a federal climate compensation fund.
The motion, which called on the federal government to establish the funding stream for councils funded through a levy on major coal, oil and gas corporations, passed at this week’s council meeting.
Deputy Lord Mayor Zelinda Sherlock said as climate change accelerates, the City of Hobart would be required to spend more on climate management and adaptation actions like road repairs, storm water infrastructure upgrades, and community education on disaster preparedness.
The fund would mean the burden of implementing these measures would not fall on ratepayers.
“(Ratepayers) are already feeling the pinch of increasing insurance premiums and grocery bills associated with climate change,” Ms Sherlock said.
“It’s a much fairer outcome for polluters to pay for the damage they’ve already caused than for our communities to pay through rate rises.” Australia Institute data shows insured losses from climate-related disasters have risen more than 12 times in 20 years, while local government revenues have grown threefold over the same period.
Hobart, Ms Sherlock said, faced the “triple threat” of bushfires, storms, and floods. The motion also calls on the federal government to establish a parliamentary inquiry into the adequacy of the 2025 climate change National Adaptation Plan, which critics argue inadequately funds local governments to deliver mandated disaster management actions.
